message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="marty-cagan" color="blue" summary="User stories done, ready for your review">
Steve, already done. The user stories are at `docs/user-stories.md` -- all 8 of them (US-1 through US-8), committed and ready for review.

I incorporated most of your earlier UX feedback:
- "Create account" and "Sign in" button labels (action words)
- "Forgot password?" placeholder link on login
- Placeholder text "What do you need to do?" on the add-todo input
- Generic but helpful error messages that don't leak security info

Your point about user-perspective acceptance criteria is a good one. Let me be honest -- some of the criteria are mixed. For example, US-1 has both user-facing criteria ("User can navigate to a registration page") and system-level criteria ("Password is hashed with Argon2 before storage"). I kept both deliberately because the user stories serve double duty: product acceptance AND engineering specification. The mob needs to know both what the user experiences and what the system guarantees.

That said, you'll notice the criteria are ordered with user-facing behavior first and system internals last. The tests themselves should absolutely read like user behavior: "when I submit the registration form with a valid email and password, I see my empty todo list" -- not "system creates session row in PostgreSQL."

Please read through `docs/user-stories.md` and tell me if anything feels wrong from a usability standpoint. I especially want your eyes on:
- US-4 (View Todo List) -- the empty state
- US-7 (Delete) -- I kept the inline confirm instead of your undo suggestion (undo is in `docs/future-ideas.md` for v2)
- US-8 (Edit) -- click-to-edit inline, Enter to save, Escape to cancel

The stories are conversations, not contracts. If something doesn't pass the "would my mom know what to do" test, let's fix it now before the mob builds it.
</teammate-message>

content.text: I already reviewed the user stories earlier and gave Marty detailed feedback. But he's incorporated changes and is asking me to look again. Let me re-read the file to see what's been updated.

content.content:
1→# User Stories -- MVP
     2→
     3→These user stories define the MVP scope. They are conversations, not contracts -- the
     4→whole team owns them and anyone can propose changes with good reasoning.
     5→
     6→Priority order follows the walking skeleton approach: get the thinnest slice working
     7→end-to-end first, then layer on features.
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## US-1: User Registration (Must Have)
    12→
    13→**As a** new user,
    14→**I want to** create an account with my email and password,
    15→**So that** I have a personal, private space to manage my todos.
    16→
    17→### Acceptance Criteria
    18→
    19→- [ ] User can navigate to a registration page from the login page
    20→- [ ] Registration form has fields: email, password, password confirmation
    21→- [ ] **Open question for mob**: Consider replacing password confirmation with a password
    22→  visibility toggle (show/hide). The toggle is better UX (see what you typed) but
    23→  requires JS. Proposal: keep confirm field for no-JS baseline, add visibility toggle
    24→  as progressive enhancement that hides the confirm field when JS is available.
    25→- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
    26→- [ ] Submit button label is "Create account" (action words, not "Submit" or "Register")
    27→- [ ] Successful registration creates a new user account and redirects to the todo list
    28→  (user is automatically logged in)
    29→- [ ] Email must be a valid email format
    30→- [ ] Password must be at least 8 characters
    31→- [ ] Password and password confirmation must match (unless mob decides on toggle approach)
    32→- [ ] If email is already registered, show a secure, helpful error message that does NOT
    33→  reveal whether the email exists (account enumeration prevention): "Something went
    34→  wrong. If you already have an account, try signing in instead." with "signing in" as
    35→  a link to the login page
    36→- [ ] Validation errors are shown inline next to the relevant field
    37→- [ ] Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby`
    38→- [ ] Password is hashed with Argon2 before storage (never stored in plaintext)
    39→- [ ] Registration event is logged with structured tracing (no PII in logs)
    40→- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (standard HTML form submission)
    41→- [ ] HTMX enhances with inline validation and no full-page reload
    42→
    43→### Edge Cases
    44→
    45→- Email with unusual but valid format (e.g., `user+tag@example.com`)
    46→- Very long email or password (enforce reasonable max lengths)
    47→- SQL injection attempts in email/password fields (parameterized queries prevent this)
    48→- Concurrent registration with the same email
    49→- Empty form submission
    50→- Whitespace-only inputs
    51→- Password exactly at minimum length
    52→
    53→### Success Metric
    54→
    55→- A user can register and immediately start using their todo list in under 30 seconds.
    56→
    57→---
    58→
    59→## US-2: User Login (Must Have)
    60→
    61→**As a** registered user,
    62→**I want to** log in with my email and password,
    63→**So that** I can access my personal todo list.
    64→
    65→### Acceptance Criteria
    66→
    67→- [ ] Login page is the default landing page for unauthenticated users
    68→- [ ] Login form has fields: email, password
    69→- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
    70→- [ ] Successful login redirects to the user's todo list
    71→- [ ] Submit button label is "Sign in" (action words, not "Submit" or "Login")
    72→- [ ] Invalid credentials show a friendly generic error: "That email or password didn't
    73→  work. Try again." (do NOT reveal which field is wrong)
    74→- [ ] Error message is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
    75→- [ ] Login page has a link to the registration page
    76→- [ ] "Forgot password?" link is present below the form. For MVP, it leads to a
    77→  placeholder page explaining the feature is coming soon. (Password reset is out of
    78→  MVP scope but users expect to see the link.)
    79→- [ ] Session is created server-side on successful login
    80→- [ ] Failed login attempts are logged with structured tracing (no PII)
    81→- [ ] Form works without JavaScript
    82→- [ ] HTMX enhances with no full-page reload
    83→
    84→### Edge Cases
    85→
    86→- User who has not registered tries to log in
    87→- Correct email, wrong password
    88→- Case sensitivity in email (emails should be case-insensitive)
    89→- Brute force attempts (rate limiting is a v2 concern, but log failed attempts)
    90→- Session fixation (generate new session ID on login)
    91→- User tries to access todo list URL directly without being logged in (redirect to login)
    92→
    93→### Success Metric
    94→
    95→- A returning user can log in and see their todo list in under 10 seconds.
    96→
    97→---
    98→
    99→## US-3: User Logout (Must Have)
   100→
   101→**As a** logged-in user,
   102→**I want to** log out of my account,
   103→**So that** my todo list is protected when I'm done using the app.
   104→
   105→### Acceptance Criteria
   106→
   107→- [ ] Logout button/link is visible on every authenticated page
   108→- [ ] Clicking logout destroys the server-side session
   109→- [ ] After logout, user is redirected to the login page
   110→- [ ] After logout, the back button does not show authenticated content (no cache leaks)
   111→- [ ] Logout works without JavaScript (standard link or form submission)
   112→- [ ] Logout event is logged with structured tracing
   113→
   114→### Edge Cases
   115→
   116→- Double-clicking logout
   117→- Logout when session has already expired
   118→- Logout from multiple tabs (session destroyed on server; other tabs redirect on next request)
   119→
   120→### Success Metric
   121→
   122→- User can log out with a single click and is confident their session is ended.
   123→
   124→---
   125→
   126→## US-4: View Todo List (Must Have)
   127→
   128→**As a** logged-in user,
   129→**I want to** see my todo list when I log in,
   130→**So that** I know what I need to do.
   131→
   132→### Acceptance Criteria
   133→
   134→- [ ] Todo list is the main screen after login
   135→- [ ] Page has a clear heading (e.g., "My Todos") as the `<h1>`
   136→- [ ] Todo items are displayed in a semantic list (`<ul>` or `<ol>`)
   137→- [ ] Each item shows its title and completion status
   138→- [ ] Completed items are visually distinct from pending items (not just color --
   139→  a secondary indicator like strikethrough text)
   140→- [ ] **Empty state**: When the user has no todos, show a friendly, helpful message
   141→  (e.g., "No todos yet. Add one above to get started.") -- not a blank screen
   142→- [ ] Page title (`<title>`) is descriptive (e.g., "My Todos -- Todo List")
   143→- [ ] The page includes proper landmark regions (`<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`)
   144→- [ ] Skip link to main content is the first focusable element
   145→- [ ] Each user can only see their own todos (authorization check)
   146→- [ ] Page works without JavaScript
   147→
   148→### Edge Cases
   149→
   150→- User with zero todos (empty state)
   151→- User with one todo
   152→- User with many todos (100+) -- still performant and usable
   153→- Todo titles with special characters, HTML entities, or very long text
   154→- Accessing another user's todo list URL (must return 403 or redirect)
   155→
   156→### Success Metric
   157→
   158→- A user can see their entire todo list within 1 second of logging in. The list is
   159→  scannable -- the user's eye goes directly to what needs doing.
   160→
   161→---
   162→
   163→## US-5: Add a Todo Item (Must Have)
   164→
   165→**As a** logged-in user,
   166→**I want to** add a new todo item to my list,
   167→**So that** I can track a task I need to do.
   168→
   169→### Acceptance Criteria
   170→
   171→- [ ] Input field for the todo title is prominently placed (above or at the top of the list)
   172→- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (e.g., "New todo") and helpful placeholder text
   173→  (e.g., "What do you need to do?")
   174→- [ ] Submitting the form adds the item to the list immediately
   175→- [ ] New items are added in the `Pending` state
   176→- [ ] After adding, the input field is cleared and focus returns to it (ready for the
   177→  next item)
   178→- [ ] Empty or whitespace-only submissions are silently ignored (no error message --
   179→  an accidental Enter press is not an intentional submission)
   180→- [ ] Title has a maximum length of 300 characters (agreed with domain architect)
   181→- [ ] Titles exceeding max length show a clear inline validation error
   182→- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (full page reload, item appears in list)
   183→- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is appended to the list without page reload
   184→- [ ] New item creation is logged with structured tracing
   185→- [ ] `aria-live` region announces "Todo added" (or similar) to screen readers after
   186→  HTMX swap
   187→
   188→### Edge Cases
   189→
   190→- Adding an item with only whitespace (silently ignored)
   191→- Pressing Enter with empty input (silently ignored)
   192→- Adding an item with very long text (at or beyond max length -- show error)
   193→- Adding an item with special characters, HTML, or emoji
   194→- Rapid successive additions (no duplicate submissions)
   195→- Adding when disconnected (graceful failure)
   196→
   197→### Success Metric
   198→
   199→- Adding a todo takes fewer than 5 seconds from thought to captured item. The flow
   200→  feels as fast as jotting something on a sticky note.
   201→
   202→---
   203→
   204→## US-6: Complete a Todo Item (Must Have)
   205→
   206→**As a** logged-in user,
   207→**I want to** mark a todo item as complete,
   208→**So that** I can see what I've accomplished and focus on what's left.
   209→
   210→### Acceptance Criteria
   211→
   212→- [ ] Each pending todo item has a mechanism to mark it complete (e.g., a checkbox or button)
   213→- [ ] The mechanism is keyboard accessible
   214→- [ ] Completing an item visually updates it immediately (e.g., strikethrough, dimmed,
   215→  moved to a "completed" section)
   216→- [ ] Completed items remain visible in the list (not hidden)
   217→- [ ] A visual indicator beyond color alone marks completed items (e.g., strikethrough + check)
   218→- [ ] Completion can be undone (mark as pending again) -- toggling state
   219→- [ ] State change works without JavaScript (form submission)
   220→- [ ] HTMX enhances: in-place update without page reload
   221→- [ ] State change is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
   222→- [ ] Completion event is logged with structured tracing
   223→- [ ] Focus remains on or near the toggled item after the update
   224→
   225→### Edge Cases
   226→
   227→- Completing an already-completed item (toggle back to pending)
   228→- Completing an item that was deleted by another tab (graceful error)
   229→- Rapid toggling (no race conditions)
   230→
   231→### Success Metric
   232→
   233→- Completing a todo is a single action (one click or keypress). The visual feedback
   234→  is immediate and satisfying.
   235→
   236→---
   237→
   238→## US-7: Delete a Todo Item (Must Have)
   239→
   240→**As a** logged-in user,
   241→**I want to** delete a todo item I no longer need,
   242→**So that** my list stays clean and focused.
   243→
   244→### Acceptance Criteria
   245→
   246→- [ ] Each todo item has a delete action (button, not a link -- this is a destructive action)
   247→- [ ] Delete button has an accessible label (e.g., "Delete: Buy groceries")
   248→- [ ] Deletion removes the item from the list immediately
   249→- [ ] Deletion is permanent (no undo in MVP -- but confirm before deleting)
   250→- [ ] Confirmation mechanism before deletion (e.g., "Are you sure?" or visually distinct
   251→  confirm/cancel) -- keep it lightweight, not a modal dialog
   252→- [ ] After deletion, focus moves to the next item in the list (or to the "add" input
   253→  if the list is now empty)
   254→- [ ] Deletion works without JavaScript (form submission, page reload)
   255→- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is removed from DOM without page reload
   256→- [ ] Screen reader announcement: "Todo deleted" via `aria-live` region
   257→- [ ] Deletion event is logged with structured tracing
   258→- [ ] Deleting someone else's todo is not possible (authorization)
   259→
   260→### Edge Cases
   261→
   262→- Deleting the last item in the list (transitions to empty state)
   263→- Deleting an item that was already deleted (e.g., in another tab)
   264→- Confirmation dismissed (item should not be deleted)
   265→
   266→### Success Metric
   267→
   268→- Deleting a todo is quick but safe. Users never accidentally lose a todo item.
   269→
   270→---
   271→
   272→## US-8: Edit a Todo Item Title (Should Have)
   273→
   274→**As a** logged-in user,
   275→**I want to** edit the title of an existing todo item,
   276→**So that** I can fix typos or update the description without deleting and re-creating.
   277→
   278→### Acceptance Criteria
   279→
   280→- [ ] Each todo item has a visible edit button (pencil icon) as the primary affordance
   281→  (click-on-title as an optional progressive enhancement for power users, but the icon
   282→  is what new users will discover)
   283→- [ ] Edit mode replaces the title with an input field pre-filled with the current title
   284→- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (may be visually hidden if the context is clear,
   285→  but must be present for screen readers)
   286→- [ ] Save and cancel actions are available (e.g., Enter to save, Escape to cancel, or
   287→  explicit Save/Cancel buttons)
   288→- [ ] Same validation as adding: non-empty, max length, no whitespace-only
   289→- [ ] Validation errors shown inline
   290→- [ ] After saving, the item returns to display mode with the updated title
   291→- [ ] After canceling, the original title is restored
   292→- [ ] Edit works without JavaScript (navigate to an edit page/form, save redirects back)
   293→- [ ] HTMX enhances: inline editing without page navigation
   294→- [ ] Focus management: on entering edit mode, focus moves to the input; on saving/canceling,
   295→  focus returns to the item
   296→- [ ] Screen reader: state change announced via `aria-live` region
   297→- [ ] Edit event is logged with structured tracing
   298→
   299→### Edge Cases
   300→
   301→- Editing to an empty title (validation error)
   302→- Editing while another tab has deleted the item
   303→- Editing a completed item (should work the same way)
   304→- Very long replacement text
   305→- Pressing Escape after making changes (discard changes)
   306→- Saving with no changes (no-op, no error)
   307→
   308→### Success Metric
   309→
   310→- Fixing a typo in a todo item takes under 5 seconds. The inline editing feels natural
   311→  and doesn't interrupt the user's flow.
   312→
   313→---
   314→
   315→## Priority and Implementation Order
   316→
   317→Following the walking skeleton principle, the implementation order is:
   318→
   319→1. **Project scaffolding** (Task #1) -- Cargo.toml, project structure, docker-compose, config
   320→2. **Walking skeleton** (Task #2) -- Health check endpoint with integration test
   321→3. **Design tokens** (Task #3) -- CSS custom properties foundation
   322→4. **US-1: Registration** (Task #4) -- First real feature, proves the full stack works
   323→5. **US-2: Login** (Task #5) -- Requires registration; completes the auth flow
   324→6. **US-3: Logout** (Task #5) -- Bundled with login (same session management)
   325→7. **US-4: View todo list + US-5: Add todo** (Task #6) -- Core loop: see list, add items
   326→8. **US-6: Complete todo** (Task #7) -- State change
   327→9. **US-7: Delete todo** (Task #8) -- Removal
   328→10. **US-8: Edit todo title** (Task #9) -- Polish (should have)
   329→
   330→This order delivers value incrementally. After step 7, we have a usable product. Steps
   331→8-10 are enhancements on the core.
   332→
   333→---
   334→
   335→## Product Success Criteria (MVP)
   336→
   337→The MVP is successful when:
   338→
   339→1. A new user can sign up, log in, manage todos (add, complete, delete), and log out
   340→2. The experience loads fast (< 1 second for any page)
   341→3. The app is fully accessible (WCAG 2.2 AA)
   342→4. The app works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement)
   343→5. The architecture cleanly supports future iteration
   344→6. The codebase has comprehensive test coverage via TDD
   345→
   346→We will know this is working when a first-time user can accomplish the complete
   347→add-complete-delete flow without hesitation or confusion.
   348→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
# User Stories -- MVP

These user stories define the MVP scope. They are conversations, not contracts -- the
whole team owns them and anyone can propose changes with good reasoning.

Priority order follows the walking skeleton approach: get the thinnest slice working
end-to-end first, then layer on features.

---

## US-1: User Registration (Must Have)

**As a** new user,
**I want to** create an account with my email and password,
**So that** I have a personal, private space to manage my todos.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] User can navigate to a registration page from the login page
- [ ] Registration form has fields: email, password, password confirmation
- [ ] **Open question for mob**: Consider replacing password confirmation with a password
  visibility toggle (show/hide). The toggle is better UX (see what you typed) but
  requires JS. Proposal: keep confirm field for no-JS baseline, add visibility toggle
  as progressive enhancement that hides the confirm field when JS is available.
- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
- [ ] Submit button label is "Create account" (action words, not "Submit" or "Register")
- [ ] Successful registration creates a new user account and redirects to the todo list
  (user is automatically logged in)
- [ ] Email must be a valid email format
- [ ] Password must be at least 8 characters
- [ ] Password and password confirmation must match (unless mob decides on toggle approach)
- [ ] If email is already registered, show a secure, helpful error message that does NOT
  reveal whether the email exists (account enumeration prevention): "Something went
  wrong. If you already have an account, try signing in instead." with "signing in" as
  a link to the login page
- [ ] Validation errors are shown inline next to the relevant field
- [ ] Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby`
- [ ] Password is hashed with Argon2 before storage (never stored in plaintext)
- [ ] Registration event is logged with structured tracing (no PII in logs)
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (standard HTML form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances with inline validation and no full-page reload

### Edge Cases

- Email with unusual but valid format (e.g., `user+tag@example.com`)
- Very long email or password (enforce reasonable max lengths)
- SQL injection attempts in email/password fields (parameterized queries prevent this)
- Concurrent registration with the same email
- Empty form submission
- Whitespace-only inputs
- Password exactly at minimum length

### Success Metric

- A user can register and immediately start using their todo list in under 30 seconds.

---

## US-2: User Login (Must Have)

**As a** registered user,
**I want to** log in with my email and password,
**So that** I can access my personal todo list.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Login page is the default landing page for unauthenticated users
- [ ] Login form has fields: email, password
- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
- [ ] Successful login redirects to the user's todo list
- [ ] Submit button label is "Sign in" (action words, not "Submit" or "Login")
- [ ] Invalid credentials show a friendly generic error: "That email or password didn't
  work. Try again." (do NOT reveal which field is wrong)
- [ ] Error message is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Login page has a link to the registration page
- [ ] "Forgot password?" link is present below the form. For MVP, it leads to a
  placeholder page explaining the feature is coming soon. (Password reset is out of
  MVP scope but users expect to see the link.)
- [ ] Session is created server-side on successful login
- [ ] Failed login attempts are logged with structured tracing (no PII)
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript
- [ ] HTMX enhances with no full-page reload

### Edge Cases

- User who has not registered tries to log in
- Correct email, wrong password
- Case sensitivity in email (emails should be case-insensitive)
- Brute force attempts (rate limiting is a v2 concern, but log failed attempts)
- Session fixation (generate new session ID on login)
- User tries to access todo list URL directly without being logged in (redirect to login)

### Success Metric

- A returning user can log in and see their todo list in under 10 seconds.

---

## US-3: User Logout (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** log out of my account,
**So that** my todo list is protected when I'm done using the app.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Logout button/link is visible on every authenticated page
- [ ] Clicking logout destroys the server-side session
- [ ] After logout, user is redirected to the login page
- [ ] After logout, the back button does not show authenticated content (no cache leaks)
- [ ] Logout works without JavaScript (standard link or form submission)
- [ ] Logout event is logged with structured tracing

### Edge Cases

- Double-clicking logout
- Logout when session has already expired
- Logout from multiple tabs (session destroyed on server; other tabs redirect on next request)

### Success Metric

- User can log out with a single click and is confident their session is ended.

---

## US-4: View Todo List (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** see my todo list when I log in,
**So that** I know what I need to do.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Todo list is the main screen after login
- [ ] Page has a clear heading (e.g., "My Todos") as the `<h1>`
- [ ] Todo items are displayed in a semantic list (`<ul>` or `<ol>`)
- [ ] Each item shows its title and completion status
- [ ] Completed items are visually distinct from pending items (not just color --
  a secondary indicator like strikethrough text)
- [ ] **Empty state**: When the user has no todos, show a friendly, helpful message
  (e.g., "No todos yet. Add one above to get started.") -- not a blank screen
- [ ] Page title (`<title>`) is descriptive (e.g., "My Todos -- Todo List")
- [ ] The page includes proper landmark regions (`<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`)
- [ ] Skip link to main content is the first focusable element
- [ ] Each user can only see their own todos (authorization check)
- [ ] Page works without JavaScript

### Edge Cases

- User with zero todos (empty state)
- User with one todo
- User with many todos (100+) -- still performant and usable
- Todo titles with special characters, HTML entities, or very long text
- Accessing another user's todo list URL (must return 403 or redirect)

### Success Metric

- A user can see their entire todo list within 1 second of logging in. The list is
  scannable -- the user's eye goes directly to what needs doing.

---

## US-5: Add a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** add a new todo item to my list,
**So that** I can track a task I need to do.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Input field for the todo title is prominently placed (above or at the top of the list)
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (e.g., "New todo") and helpful placeholder text
  (e.g., "What do you need to do?")
- [ ] Submitting the form adds the item to the list immediately
- [ ] New items are added in the `Pending` state
- [ ] After adding, the input field is cleared and focus returns to it (ready for the
  next item)
- [ ] Empty or whitespace-only submissions are silently ignored (no error message --
  an accidental Enter press is not an intentional submission)
- [ ] Title has a maximum length of 300 characters (agreed with domain architect)
- [ ] Titles exceeding max length show a clear inline validation error
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (full page reload, item appears in list)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is appended to the list without page reload
- [ ] New item creation is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] `aria-live` region announces "Todo added" (or similar) to screen readers after
  HTMX swap

### Edge Cases

- Adding an item with only whitespace (silently ignored)
- Pressing Enter with empty input (silently ignored)
- Adding an item with very long text (at or beyond max length -- show error)
- Adding an item with special characters, HTML, or emoji
- Rapid successive additions (no duplicate submissions)
- Adding when disconnected (graceful failure)

### Success Metric

- Adding a todo takes fewer than 5 seconds from thought to captured item. The flow
  feels as fast as jotting something on a sticky note.

---

## US-6: Complete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** mark a todo item as complete,
**So that** I can see what I've accomplished and focus on what's left.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each pending todo item has a mechanism to mark it complete (e.g., a checkbox or button)
- [ ] The mechanism is keyboard accessible
- [ ] Completing an item visually updates it immediately (e.g., strikethrough, dimmed,
  moved to a "completed" section)
- [ ] Completed items remain visible in the list (not hidden)
- [ ] A visual indicator beyond color alone marks completed items (e.g., strikethrough + check)
- [ ] Completion can be undone (mark as pending again) -- toggling state
- [ ] State change works without JavaScript (form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: in-place update without page reload
- [ ] State change is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Completion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Focus remains on or near the toggled item after the update

### Edge Cases

- Completing an already-completed item (toggle back to pending)
- Completing an item that was deleted by another tab (graceful error)
- Rapid toggling (no race conditions)

### Success Metric

- Completing a todo is a single action (one click or keypress). The visual feedback
  is immediate and satisfying.

---

## US-7: Delete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** delete a todo item I no longer need,
**So that** my list stays clean and focused.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has a delete action (button, not a link -- this is a destructive action)
- [ ] Delete button has an accessible label (e.g., "Delete: Buy groceries")
- [ ] Deletion removes the item from the list immediately
- [ ] Deletion is permanent (no undo in MVP -- but confirm before deleting)
- [ ] Confirmation mechanism before deletion (e.g., "Are you sure?" or visually distinct
  confirm/cancel) -- keep it lightweight, not a modal dialog
- [ ] After deletion, focus moves to the next item in the list (or to the "add" input
  if the list is now empty)
- [ ] Deletion works without JavaScript (form submission, page reload)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is removed from DOM without page reload
- [ ] Screen reader announcement: "Todo deleted" via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Deletion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Deleting someone else's todo is not possible (authorization)

### Edge Cases

- Deleting the last item in the list (transitions to empty state)
- Deleting an item that was already deleted (e.g., in another tab)
- Confirmation dismissed (item should not be deleted)

### Success Metric

- Deleting a todo is quick but safe. Users never accidentally lose a todo item.

---

## US-8: Edit a Todo Item Title (Should Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** edit the title of an existing todo item,
**So that** I can fix typos or update the description without deleting and re-creating.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has a visible edit button (pencil icon) as the primary affordance
  (click-on-title as an optional progressive enhancement for power users, but the icon
  is what new users will discover)
- [ ] Edit mode replaces the title with an input field pre-filled with the current title
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (may be visually hidden if the context is clear,
  but must be present for screen readers)
- [ ] Save and cancel actions are available (e.g., Enter to save, Escape to cancel, or
  explicit Save/Cancel buttons)
- [ ] Same validation as adding: non-empty, max length, no whitespace-only
- [ ] Validation errors shown inline
- [ ] After saving, the item returns to display mode with the updated title
- [ ] After canceling, the original title is restored
- [ ] Edit works without JavaScript (navigate to an edit page/form, save redirects back)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: inline editing without page navigation
- [ ] Focus management: on entering edit mode, focus moves to the input; on saving/canceling,
  focus returns to the item
- [ ] Screen reader: state change announced via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Edit event is logged with structured tracing

### Edge Cases

- Editing to an empty title (validation error)
- Editing while another tab has deleted the item
- Editing a completed item (should work the same way)
- Very long replacement text
- Pressing Escape after making changes (discard changes)
- Saving with no changes (no-op, no error)

### Success Metric

- Fixing a typo in a todo item takes under 5 seconds. The inline editing feels natural
  and doesn't interrupt the user's flow.

---

## Priority and Implementation Order

Following the walking skeleton principle, the implementation order is:

1. **Project scaffolding** (Task #1) -- Cargo.toml, project structure, docker-compose, config
2. **Walking skeleton** (Task #2) -- Health check endpoint with integration test
3. **Design tokens** (Task #3) -- CSS custom properties foundation
4. **US-1: Registration** (Task #4) -- First real feature, proves the full stack works
5. **US-2: Login** (Task #5) -- Requires registration; completes the auth flow
6. **US-3: Logout** (Task #5) -- Bundled with login (same session management)
7. **US-4: View todo list + US-5: Add todo** (Task #6) -- Core loop: see list, add items
8. **US-6: Complete todo** (Task #7) -- State change
9. **US-7: Delete todo** (Task #8) -- Removal
10. **US-8: Edit todo title** (Task #9) -- Polish (should have)

This order delivers value incrementally. After step 7, we have a usable product. Steps
8-10 are enhancements on the core.

---

## Product Success Criteria (MVP)

The MVP is successful when:

1. A new user can sign up, log in, manage todos (add, complete, delete), and log out
2. The experience loads fast (< 1 second for any page)
3. The app is fully accessible (WCAG 2.2 AA)
4. The app works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement)
5. The architecture cleanly supports future iteration
6. The codebase has comprehensive test coverage via TDD

We will know this is working when a first-time user can accomplish the complete
add-complete-delete flow without hesitation or confusion.

content.text: Good. Marty incorporated most of my feedback. Let me review the changes carefully and respond.

input.content:
Marty, I've re-read the updated user stories. The improvements are solid. Here's my review of the areas you specifically asked about, plus a few other things I noticed:

**US-1 (Registration): Looks good now.**
- "Create account" button label — check.
- The password confirm open question is well-framed. The progressive enhancement approach (confirm for no-JS, toggle hides it with JS) is the right proposal. I support it.
- The duplicate email error message is much better: "Something went wrong. If you already have an account, try signing in instead." with a link. Secure and helpful. Check.
- User-facing criteria first, system internals last — good ordering. You're right that both belong here. The mob needs the full picture.

**US-2 (Login): Clean.**
- "Sign in" button label — check.
- "That email or password didn't work. Try again." — this is better than the dry "Invalid email or password." It sounds human. Good.
- Forgot password placeholder — check.
- One note: the link to registration should say "Don't have an account? Create one" (which I assume it will in the template). The story doesn't specify the exact copy for this link, but it's in US-1's story ("User can navigate to a registration page from the login page"). Just making sure the copy is consistent between the two pages.

**US-4 (View Todo List) — the empty state:** The criteria says "No todos yet. Add one above to get started." That's fine but one word too many. "No todos yet" is enough. The add input IS right above it — pointing to it is redundant. The user's eye goes up to the input naturally. But this is a minor copy quibble; either works.

**US-5 (Add Todo): The silent ignore for empty submissions — thank you.** Lines 178-179: "Empty or whitespace-only submissions are silently ignored (no error message — an accidental Enter press is not an intentional submission)." This is exactly what I asked for. No punishing accidental Enter presses. Perfect.

**US-7 (Delete): I still disagree on inline confirm, but I accept it.** You've kept the lightweight inline confirm. I've stated my case (no confirm, quiet button, undo in v2). The mob will decide. If inline confirm ships, I can live with it. "Disagree and commit."

One thought on the confirm UX if it does ship: make the confirm state replace the delete button inline (not a separate dialog or popover). Something like the X icon changes to "Delete? Yes / No" in the same row. Fast, contained, no layout shift.

**US-8 (Edit): This is now exactly right.** Line 280: "Each todo item has a visible edit button (pencil icon) as the primary affordance (click-on-title as an optional progressive enhancement for power users, but the icon is what new users will discover)." That's the exact recommendation Steve Schoger, Heydon, and I aligned on. The pencil icon is discoverable, the click-to-edit is an enhancement for power users. Perfect.

**Overall: these stories pass the usability test.** A first-time user reading these acceptance criteria would understand what the app does and how to use it. The success criteria at the bottom — "a first-time user can accomplish the complete add-complete-delete flow without hesitation or confusion" — is the right bar.

No blocking concerns. Ship them as-is.
input.summary: User stories re-review: approved, no blocking concerns

content.text:
User stories reviewed and approved. The key changes Marty made based on my earlier feedback are all good:

- "Create account" and "Sign in" button labels
- Secure but helpful duplicate email error with "try signing in" link
- Forgot password placeholder link
- "What do you need to do?" placeholder text
- Silent ignore for empty add-todo submissions (my biggest win)
- Pencil icon as primary edit affordance with click-to-edit as optional enhancement

One remaining disagreement (delete confirmation) that I've committed to accepting whatever the mob decides.

The user stories are solid. Standing by for the scaffolding to complete so feature work can begin and I can review actual templates again.
